Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108683

Relevance: 0.586 (consider)

The mechanism of Neoarchean crustal growth in the Yinshan Block of the western North China Craton remains controversial. Previous studies have primarily focused on Neoarchean granitic rocks, with limited investigation of metavolcanic rocks, constraining our understanding of regional magmatism. In this study, we present systematic zircon UPb geochronology, whole-rock geochemistry, and LuHf isotope analyses of the Neoarchean metavolcanic rocks exposed in the Xiwulanbulang area of the Yinshan Block, aiming to constrain their formation ages, petrogenesis, and tectonic setting. Zircon UPb dating indicates that these metavolcanic rocks were emplaced at 2.58–2.55 Ga. Geochemically, these metavolcanic rocks exhibit affinities to mid-ocean ridge basalts (MORB), characterized by depleted mantle signatures with magma sources derived from spinel-garnet lherzolite (approximately 10–70% partial melting). Although such MORB-like geochemical features are typically associated with divergent plate boundaries, in the Archean context they do not necessarily imply the existence of modern-style mid-ocean ridges. Instead, these features can be reconciled with a mantle plume regime, in which large-scale decompression melting beneath a mantle plume head produces basaltic magmas that share similar depleted mantle geochemical fingerprints. Whole-rock geochemical data and zircon ε Hf(t) values (ranging from +1.6 to +8.0) further indicate derivation from partial melting of a depleted mantle source without significant crustal contamination. Thus, the MORB-like compositions of these rocks are best interpreted as the product of a mantle plume–related melting event, rather than evidence for plate divergence or ridge subduction. Additionally, these metavolcanic rocks were likely formed in a tectonic setting involving mantle plume-ridge interaction. Therefore, we propose that the Yinshan Block experienced a crustal accretion event dominated by mantle plume activity during the Neoarchean. This understanding provides new insights into the early crustal formation mechanisms of the North China Craton.

item.title: Metallogenic age, material sources, and ore-forming process of the Donggou VMS-type zinc deposit in the Qimantag Area, East Kunlun Orogenic Belt
item.dc:creator: Haiming Guo
Xiaoyong Yang
Huishan Zhang
Guowen Miao
Bin Wang
Yu Yang
Yiming Chen
Yunping Li
Fukun Chen
item.description: The Donggou zinc deposit is the first discovered volcanogenic massive sulfide (VMS)-type lead–zinc deposit in the East Kunlun orogen, holding significant scientific value and economic potential. As a newly identified deposit type in the region, key scientific issues such as its metallogenic age, sources of ore-forming materials, ore-forming conditions, and genetic mechanism remain unclear. This study systematically analyzes the major and trace element compositions of sphalerite from different ore-forming stages within the main mineralization period, the in situ S-Pb isotopic characteristics of major metallic sulfides, and zircon U-Pb dating of the host rocks. The aim is to delineate the ore-forming process and elucidate the genetic mechanism of the deposit. Results indicate that the main mineralization period of the Donggou deposit can be divided into three stages: stockwork stage (I), massive sulfide stage (II), and laminated stage (III). The trace element compositions of sphalerite from these stages (Sp1, Sp2, Sp3) show significant differences: Sp1 is relatively enriched in Cu, In, Se, and Co; Sp2 is enriched in Mn, Ag, As, and Pb; and Sp3 is characterized by enrichment in Sb and Ga. Temperature estimates based on the GGIMFIS geothermometer for sphalerite are 314-361°C (avg. 334°C) for Sp1, 296-336°C (avg. 317°C) for Sp2, and 286-334°C (avg. 308°C) for Sp3. Corresponding sulfur fugacity (lg f s 2 ) ranges are −6.79 to −4.93 for Sp1, −7.60 to −5.89 for Sp2, and −8.10 to −6.23 for Sp3. These indicate that the ore-forming fluids were generated under medium‑ to high‑temperature and relatively high sulfur fugacity conditions. From early to late mineralization, temperature remained generally stable, while sulfur fugacity gradually decreased. In situ δ34S values of sulfides (−3.59‰ to 6.24‰) suggest that sulfur was primarily derived from the degassing of a shallow magma chamber or the leaching of wall rocks, with minor incorporation of seawater sulfate sulfur formed via thermochemical sulfate reduction (TSR). Lead isotope compositions of galena (208Pb/204Pb = 37.980–38.021, 207Pb/204Pb = 15.575–15.613, 206Pb/204Pb = 18.210–18.261) display characteristics of a mixture derived from basement and wall rocks. Zircon U-Pb dating of the host rock yields an age of 446 ± 1.5 Ma, indicating that the deposit formed during the Late Ordovician subduction of the Qimantag Ocean. In summary, this study delineates for the first time the metallogenic age, material sources, and evolution of physicochemical conditions of the ore-forming fluids for the Donggou VMS-type zinc deposit, providing a detailed reconstruction of the ore-forming process. These findings are significant for understanding VMS-type mineralization in the East Kunlun orogen and for guiding regional mineral resources exploration. Published: 2026-06-17T23:34:28+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.583 (consider)

These findings are significant for understanding VMS-type mineralization in the East Kunlun orogen and for guiding regional mineral resources exploration.

item.title: Linking zircon (re-)crystallization to partial melting in migmatitic orthogneiss: Constraints on the tectonometamorphic evolution of the Upper Greater Himalayan Sequence, Western Arunachal Himalaya, NE India
item.dc:creator: Md. Sunny Hussain
Purbajyoti Phukon
John Cottle
Morgan Adamson
Kalpajyoti Mazumdar
item.description: Migmatitic orthogneisses provide significant insights into the relationships between partial melting, and zircon (re-)crystallization, and are the key to understanding crustal anatexis. This study focuses on migmatitic orthogneisses from the middle and upper Greater Himalayan Sequence (GHSM; GHSU) across the Zimithang Thrust (ZT) in the western Arunachal Himalaya (WAH), NE India, employing field observations, microtextures, mineral chemistry, phase equilibria modeling, and zircon petrochronology to understand partial melting processes in the Arunachal Himalaya and their broader tectonic implications. The ZT delineates a metamorphic break, separating an aluminosilicate-absent orthogneiss with incipient partial melting in the footwall from a kyanite-sillimanite-bearing migmatitic orthogneiss with metatexite textures in the hanging wall. The immediate footwall rocks reached peak P-T conditions of 1.05–1.00 GPa and 700–680 °C, generating ~6 vol% melt, before decompressing to 0.93–0.88 GPa and 650–640 °C, where melt is believed to crystallize completely. No prograde metamorphic history is preserved, and muscovite remained stable, consistent with limited melt production (~6 vol%) and closed-system behavior. In contrast, hanging-wall rocks record a clockwise P–T trajectory, from prograde burial at 0.72–0.60 GPa and 655–645 °C to peak suprasolidus conditions of 1.25–0.88 GPa and 820–780 °C. Muscovite dehydration melting produced ~22 vol% and ~ 29 vol% melt at high-P and high-T peak metamorphic conditions, respectively. Peak metamorphism was followed by decompression to 0.72–0.6 GPa and 700–640 °C. Zircon records three distinct age clusters in the hanging wall: 30–26, ~24, and ~ 18 Ma, signifying prograde heating, peak melting, and decompression associated with garnet resorption, respectively. Zircon in the footwall orthogneiss yield ~20 Ma rims, enriched in HREE with negative Eu anomalies, consistent with garnet re-equilibration and plagioclase growth. The findings indicate that muscovite dehydration melting started by ~30 Ma, reached peak metamorphism at 24–20 Ma, followed by the decompression at <20 Ma, which marked the initiation of the ZT. Our results reveal an early Miocene exhumation phase of the GHSU along the ZT, from 36 to 41 km to 20–24 km depth over 3–4 Myr (19–16 Ma) at rates of ~5 mm/yr, representing an out-of-sequence exhumation pulse that was followed by late Miocene movement along the ZT. This study suggests that melt-enhanced weakening, coupled with thrust activity, facilitated the extrusion of the hanging wall, providing new insights into partial melting and tectonometamorphic evolution of the GHSU in the WAH. Published: 2026-06-20T15:35:39+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.583 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Md. Sunny Hussain; Purbajyoti Phukon; John Cottle; Morgan Adamson; Kalpajyoti Mazumdar

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108682

Relevance: 0.583 (consider)

Migmatitic orthogneisses provide significant insights into the relationships between partial melting, and zircon (re-)crystallization, and are the key to understanding crustal anatexis. This study focuses on migmatitic orthogneisses from the middle and upper Greater Himalayan Sequence (GHSM; GHSU) across the Zimithang Thrust (ZT) in the western Arunachal Himalaya (WAH), NE India, employing field observations, microtextures, mineral chemistry, phase equilibria modeling, and zircon petrochronology to understand partial melting processes in the Arunachal Himalaya and their broader tectonic implications. The ZT delineates a metamorphic break, separating an aluminosilicate-absent orthogneiss with incipient partial melting in the footwall from a kyanite-sillimanite-bearing migmatitic orthogneiss with metatexite textures in the hanging wall. The immediate footwall rocks reached peak P-T conditions of 1.05–1.00 GPa and 700–680 °C, generating ~6 vol% melt, before decompressing to 0.93–0.88 GPa and 650–640 °C, where melt is believed to crystallize completely. No prograde metamorphic history is preserved, and muscovite remained stable, consistent with limited melt production (~6 vol%) and closed-system behavior. In contrast, hanging-wall rocks record a clockwise P–T trajectory, from prograde burial at 0.72–0.60 GPa and 655–645 °C to peak suprasolidus conditions of 1.25–0.88 GPa and 820–780 °C. Muscovite dehydration melting produced ~22 vol% and ~ 29 vol% melt at high-P and high-T peak metamorphic conditions, respectively. Peak metamorphism was followed by decompression to 0.72–0.6 GPa and 700–640 °C. Zircon records three distinct age clusters in the hanging wall: 30–26, ~24, and ~ 18 Ma, signifying prograde heating, peak melting, and decompression associated with garnet resorption, respectively. Zircon in the footwall orthogneiss yield ~20 Ma rims, enriched in HREE with negative Eu anomalies, consistent with garnet re-equilibration and plagioclase growth. The findings indicate that muscovite dehydration melting started by ~30 Ma, reached peak metamorphism at 24–20 Ma, followed by the decompression at <20 Ma, which marked the initiation of the ZT. Our results reveal an early Miocene exhumation phase of the GHSU along the ZT, from 36 to 41 km to 20–24 km depth over 3–4 Myr (19–16 Ma) at rates of ~5 mm/yr, representing an out-of-sequence exhumation pulse that was followed by late Miocene movement along the ZT. This study suggests that melt-enhanced weakening, coupled with thrust activity, facilitated the extrusion of the hanging wall, providing new insights into partial melting and tectonometamorphic evolution of the GHSU in the WAH.

item.title: Revisiting the formation and differentiation of Neoproterozoic continental arc crust in the western Yangtze Block, South China
item.dc:creator: Yu Zhu
Xiao-ping Xia
Feng Guo
Yan-jie Tang
Xin Zhu
Chang-xin Yin
M.P. Manu Prasanth
Ren-zhi Zhu
Min Liu
Zhuang Guo
Jiang-feng Qin
Fang-yi Zhang
Hang Yang
Shao-cong Lai
item.description: Neoproterozoic continental arc magmatism in the western Yangtze Block (South China) provides critical insights into early crust-mantle evolution during circum-Rodinia subduction-accretionary orogenesis. However, the specific processes of Neoproterozoic continental crustal growth and stratification remain unclear. Herein, we present a comprehensive synthesis of mineral chemistry, zircon U–Pb–Hf isotopes, whole-rock major and trace elements as well as SrNd isotopes from representative Neoproterozoic Igneous Intrusive Complexes (NIICs) in the western Yangtze Block, including the Tianquan (TIC; ca. 833–825 Ma) and Shimian complexes (SIC; ca. 781–779 Ma) in this study, alongside the previously reported Nanba (NIC; ca. 796–790 Ma) and Luding complexes (LIC; ca. 783–779 Ma). The NIICs comprise mafic gabbros through intermediate diorites to highly fractionated silica-rich granitoids, suggesting a coherent calc-alkaline differentiation trend. They exhibit variable but predominantly depleted whole-rock SrNd (e.g., TIC: εNd(t) = −0.08 ~ +4.55; NIC: εNd(t) = −0.60 ~ +1.91; LIC: εNd(t) = +2.35 ~ +5.10; SIC: εNd(t) = +0.50 ~ +6.07) and zircon Hf isotopes (TIC: mean εHf(t) = +7.75 ~ +11.89; NIC: mean εHf(t) = +4.33 ~ +5.66; LIC: εHf(t) = +4.03 ~ +7.69; SIC: εHf(t) = +5.69 ~ +9.81), indicating a heterogeneous mantle source metasomatized by subducted components. Combined with “crust-like” trace element signatures, systematic whole-rock compositional variations, and hornblende P-T estimates, the NIICs represent middle–upper crustal sections, which underscore a three-stage evolution for Neoproterozoic continental arc crustal growth and fractionation: (1) the generation of primary basaltic melts from heterogeneous metasomatized mantle source infiltrated by subducted components; (2) the long-lived supply of metasomatized mantle-derived melts, magma mixing, and polybaric crystallization-driven differentiation within a trans-crustal magmatic system; and (3) extraction of high-silica melts in a shallow mush reservoir. Integrated with previous studies, our findings demonstrate that this complex array of petrogenetic processes—alongside the partial melting of diverse crustal sources—ultimately governed the maturation and vertical stratification of the Neoproterozoic continental arc crust in the western Yangtze Block. Published: 2026-06-20T15:01:01+00:00 Venue: Chemical Geology Score: 0.579 (consider)

Authors: Yu Zhu; Xiao-ping Xia; Feng Guo; Yan-jie Tang; Xin Zhu; Chang-xin Yin; M.P. Manu Prasanth; Ren-zhi Zhu; Min Liu; Zhuang Guo; Jiang-feng Qin; Fang-yi Zhang; Hang Yang; Shao-cong Lai

Journal: Chemical Geology

DOI: 10.1016/j.chemgeo.2026.123573

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

Neoproterozoic continental arc magmatism in the western Yangtze Block (South China) provides critical insights into early crust-mantle evolution during circum-Rodinia subduction-accretionary orogenesis. However, the specific processes of Neoproterozoic continental crustal growth and stratification remain unclear. Herein, we present a comprehensive synthesis of mineral chemistry, zircon U–Pb–Hf isotopes, whole-rock major and trace elements as well as SrNd isotopes from representative Neoproterozoic Igneous Intrusive Complexes (NIICs) in the western Yangtze Block, including the Tianquan (TIC; ca. 833–825 Ma) and Shimian complexes (SIC; ca. 781–779 Ma) in this study, alongside the previously reported Nanba (NIC; ca. 796–790 Ma) and Luding complexes (LIC; ca. 783–779 Ma). The NIICs comprise mafic gabbros through intermediate diorites to highly fractionated silica-rich granitoids, suggesting a coherent calc-alkaline differentiation trend. They exhibit variable but predominantly depleted whole-rock SrNd (e.g., TIC: εNd(t) = −0.08 ~ +4.55; NIC: εNd(t) = −0.60 ~ +1.91; LIC: εNd(t) = +2.35 ~ +5.10; SIC: εNd(t) = +0.50 ~ +6.07) and zircon Hf isotopes (TIC: mean εHf(t) = +7.75 ~ +11.89; NIC: mean εHf(t) = +4.33 ~ +5.66; LIC: εHf(t) = +4.03 ~ +7.69; SIC: εHf(t) = +5.69 ~ +9.81), indicating a heterogeneous mantle source metasomatized by subducted components. Combined with “crust-like” trace element signatures, systematic whole-rock compositional variations, and hornblende P-T estimates, the NIICs represent middle–upper crustal sections, which underscore a three-stage evolution for Neoproterozoic continental arc crustal growth and fractionation: (1) the generation of primary basaltic melts from heterogeneous metasomatized mantle source infiltrated by subducted components; (2) the long-lived supply of metasomatized mantle-derived melts, magma mixing, and polybaric crystallization-driven differentiation within a trans-crustal magmatic system; and (3) extraction of high-silica melts in a shallow mush reservoir. Integrated with previous studies, our findings demonstrate that this complex array of petrogenetic processes—alongside the partial melting of diverse crustal sources—ultimately governed the maturation and vertical stratification of the Neoproterozoic continental arc crust in the western Yangtze Block.

item.title: Ore-forming fluid evolution linked to polymetallic mineralization within basement granitoids of the Eastern Dharwar Craton, India
item.dc:creator: Sneha Mukherjee
Gautam Ghosh
Sankar Bose
Paramita Das
Nilanjana Sorcar
item.description: Uranium–sulfide co-mineralization represents a significant process in the evolution of hydrothermal systems, often marking redox-controlled precipitation mechanisms during post-magmatic fluid activity. The present study is carried out in the Chitrial area of the Eastern Dharwar Craton (EDC), India, where uranium mineralization occurs within the ~2.51–2.52 Ga granitoids unconformably overlain by the Paleoproterozoic (~1.7–1.8 Ga) Srisailam Formation of the Cuddapah Basin. The mineralization is fracture-controlled and largely confined to the granitoid basement, where uranium occurs as uraninite and coffinite associated with pyrite, chalcopyrite, and galena within hydrothermally altered zones. Petrographic and EPMA analyses reveal multiple generations of pyrite (Py1–Py3) and distinct paragenetic stages, reflecting evolving redox and physico-chemical conditions during mineralization. Early disseminated pyrite (Py1) formed under reducing conditions, while subsequent oxidative–reductive fluctuations facilitated successive sulfide, uraninite, and coffinite precipitation. Uraninite–pyrite intergrowths and coffinite overprints suggest reduction of U6+ to U4+ mediated by pyrite and its derivatives. Phase chemistry of pyrite indicates Co variations (11–900 ppm) and sulfur-deficient compositions (Fe/S = 0.83–1.09), consistent with a hydrothermal origin under epithermal conditions. Uraninite compositions (UO2 = 86–89 wt%, PbO = 1–4 wt%) and low Th and Y contents further confirm their hydrothermal derivation and later remobilization. The paragenetic sequence from pyrite to uraninite–coffinite–autunite reflects progressive fluid evolution from reducing to oxidizing regimes. Collectively, our results indicate that post-magmatic, structurally controlled hydrothermal alteration of fertile granitoids facilitated uranium mobilization and reprecipitation, providing insights into the physico-chemical evolution of ore-forming fluids within the Chitrial granitoids of the EDC. Published: 2026-06-20T15:03:27+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.578 (consider)

Authors: Sneha Mukherjee; Gautam Ghosh; Sankar Bose; Paramita Das; Nilanjana Sorcar

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108677

Relevance: 0.578 (consider)

Uranium–sulfide co-mineralization represents a significant process in the evolution of hydrothermal systems, often marking redox-controlled precipitation mechanisms during post-magmatic fluid activity. The present study is carried out in the Chitrial area of the Eastern Dharwar Craton (EDC), India, where uranium mineralization occurs within the ~2.51–2.52 Ga granitoids unconformably overlain by the Paleoproterozoic (~1.7–1.8 Ga) Srisailam Formation of the Cuddapah Basin. The mineralization is fracture-controlled and largely confined to the granitoid basement, where uranium occurs as uraninite and coffinite associated with pyrite, chalcopyrite, and galena within hydrothermally altered zones. Petrographic and EPMA analyses reveal multiple generations of pyrite (Py1–Py3) and distinct paragenetic stages, reflecting evolving redox and physico-chemical conditions during mineralization. Early disseminated pyrite (Py1) formed under reducing conditions, while subsequent oxidative–reductive fluctuations facilitated successive sulfide, uraninite, and coffinite precipitation. Uraninite–pyrite intergrowths and coffinite overprints suggest reduction of U6+ to U4+ mediated by pyrite and its derivatives. Phase chemistry of pyrite indicates Co variations (11–900 ppm) and sulfur-deficient compositions (Fe/S = 0.83–1.09), consistent with a hydrothermal origin under epithermal conditions. Uraninite compositions (UO2 = 86–89 wt%, PbO = 1–4 wt%) and low Th and Y contents further confirm their hydrothermal derivation and later remobilization. The paragenetic sequence from pyrite to uraninite–coffinite–autunite reflects progressive fluid evolution from reducing to oxidizing regimes. Collectively, our results indicate that post-magmatic, structurally controlled hydrothermal alteration of fertile granitoids facilitated uranium mobilization and reprecipitation, providing insights into the physico-chemical evolution of ore-forming fluids within the Chitrial granitoids of the EDC.

item.title: Tellurium-rich mineralization in submarine magmatic–hydrothermal systems: Insights from the TA25 East vent field, Tonga arc
item.dc:creator: Sun Ki Choi
Sang Joon Pak
Jung-Woo Park
Jonguk Kim
Ryoung Gyun Kim
Dong-Geon Lee
item.description: Copper-rich hydrothermal chimneys from the TA25 East vent field (TA25 EVF) in the Tonga Arc contain exceptionally high Te concentrations, reaching up to 1650 ppm in bulk samples. Here, we integrate mineralogical and geochemical data to constrain the occurrence and partitioning of Te and to clarify the processes responsible for its enrichment in an arc-related submarine hydrothermal system. Tellurium is hosted both as lattice-bound Te in sulfide minerals and as discrete polymetallic tellurides and native Te. Its distribution is governed by fluid evolution during chimney growth: under relatively reduced conditions, Te is preferentially incorporated into sulfide lattices, whereas fluid–seawater mixing decreases Te solubility and promotes the precipitation of tellurides and native Te, mainly through an increase in pH and, to a lesser extent, cooling. Sulfide recrystallization further redistributes Te and promotes its local reprecipitation as discrete Te-bearing phases along the grain boundaries of earlier sulfides. Mineralogical, geochemical, and sulfur isotope evidence collectively indicates a strong magmatic volatile contribution to hydrothermal mineralization at the TA25 EVF. Key evidence includes the occurrence of enargite–tennantite, specular hematite, and telluride–kaolinite clusters; enrichment in epithermal-suite elements (Au, Ag, As, Sb, Te, Bi, and Hg); elevated pyrite Te/As (up to 0.3) and Te/Sb (up to 6.58) ratios; sulfide δ34S values (+0.85 ‰ to + 5.12 ‰) that may record seawater modification of a magmatic volatile-derived sulfur contribution; and CO2-rich (600–2500 ppm) hydrothermal plumes. Strong whole-rock associations among Au, Bi, and Te, together with polymetallic telluride assemblages and locally melt-like textures, suggest that aqueous transport may not fully account for their enrichment and that Bi–(Te)-rich melts could have played an additional role in Au scavenging. Additionally, fluid-controlled decoupling of Au from Te and Bi indicates pronounced small-scale chemical heterogeneity in hydrothermal fluids within a single vent field (∼371 m), reflecting spatially variable mixing among magmatic volatiles, hydrothermal fluids, and seawater along caldera-controlled fluid pathways. Consequently, our results underscore the importance of coupled magmatic–hydrothermal processes in driving Te enrichment in arc-related seafloor hydrothermal systems. Published: 2026-06-15T23:30:18+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.576 (consider)

Authors: Sun Ki Choi; Sang Joon Pak; Jung-Woo Park; Jonguk Kim; Ryoung Gyun Kim; Dong-Geon Lee

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107387

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Copper-rich hydrothermal chimneys from the TA25 East vent field (TA25 EVF) in the Tonga Arc contain exceptionally high Te concentrations, reaching up to 1650 ppm in bulk samples. Here, we integrate mineralogical and geochemical data to constrain the occurrence and partitioning of Te and to clarify the processes responsible for its enrichment in an arc-related submarine hydrothermal system. Tellurium is hosted both as lattice-bound Te in sulfide minerals and as discrete polymetallic tellurides and native Te. Its distribution is governed by fluid evolution during chimney growth: under relatively reduced conditions, Te is preferentially incorporated into sulfide lattices, whereas fluid–seawater mixing decreases Te solubility and promotes the precipitation of tellurides and native Te, mainly through an increase in pH and, to a lesser extent, cooling. Sulfide recrystallization further redistributes Te and promotes its local reprecipitation as discrete Te-bearing phases along the grain boundaries of earlier sulfides. Mineralogical, geochemical, and sulfur isotope evidence collectively indicates a strong magmatic volatile contribution to hydrothermal mineralization at the TA25 EVF. Key evidence includes the occurrence of enargite–tennantite, specular hematite, and telluride–kaolinite clusters; enrichment in epithermal-suite elements (Au, Ag, As, Sb, Te, Bi, and Hg); elevated pyrite Te/As (up to 0.3) and Te/Sb (up to 6.58) ratios; sulfide δ34S values (+0.85 ‰ to + 5.12 ‰) that may record seawater modification of a magmatic volatile-derived sulfur contribution; and CO2-rich (600–2500 ppm) hydrothermal plumes. Strong whole-rock associations among Au, Bi, and Te, together with polymetallic telluride assemblages and locally melt-like textures, suggest that aqueous transport may not fully account for their enrichment and that Bi–(Te)-rich melts could have played an additional role in Au scavenging. Additionally, fluid-controlled decoupling of Au from Te and Bi indicates pronounced small-scale chemical heterogeneity in hydrothermal fluids within a single vent field (∼371 m), reflecting spatially variable mixing among magmatic volatiles, hydrothermal fluids, and seawater along caldera-controlled fluid pathways. Consequently, our results underscore the importance of coupled magmatic–hydrothermal processes in driving Te enrichment in arc-related seafloor hydrothermal systems.

item.title: Implications of Cryogenian magmatism in the light of evolutionary history of the Southern Granulite Terrane, Indian shield: A case study from Sholayar Syenite Pluton
item.dc:creator: Moumita Chowdhury
Jyotisankar Ray
Debaleena Sarkar
Suresh C. Patel
J. Amal Dev
Christian Koeberl
Anindita Dey
Desikan Ramakrishnan
Rohit Pandey
Pratik Mukherjee
item.description: The Sholayar syenite pluton (10°17′59.9”N-10°18′39.8”N: 076°47′26.3″E-076°43′51.6″E) is located in the Madurai block of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT) within the Indian shield, and it is bounded by prominent shear zones in this region namely, Palghat Cauvery Shear Zone, Moyar Bhabani Shear Zone, and Achankovil Shear Zone. The Sholayar syenite stretches for about 8 km along the strike length in a WNW-ESE direction and consists of three main litho-members (namely leuco syenite, melanofelsic syenite and mela syenite) based on their petrographic characters. Geochemically, the syenite pluton exhibits a wide compositional spectrum ranging from alkalic, alkali-calcic, calcic-alkali and alkalic. Geochemical variation diagrams clearly indicate the role of differentiation from a parent magma that suffered fractional crystallization, being controlled by the constituent minerals (alkali feldspar, plagioclase, amphibole, biotite and clinopyroxene). The SrNd isotopic ratio shows a clear separation of the isotopic data sets between the mela syenite and leuco syenite + melanofelsic syenite groups. The mela syenites show higher values of εNd and lower initial Strontium-ratios, suggesting a cumulate nature which is also supported by the petrographic evidence. SrNd isotopic ratios suggest that the silica-oversaturated type was formed during crustal contamination accompanied by fractional crystallization from a parent silica- undersaturated (basanitic) stratified magma chamber, while the complementary silica- undersaturated syenite type is typically uncontaminated. Slab melting played an important role for generation of the parent magma from garnet/spinel lherzolite or Neoarchean/Paleoproterozoic protolith. The melting process was controlled by a convergent tectonic setting (close to subduction) as evident from the geochemistry and tectonic discrimination diagrams. The deduced UPb zircon age (706.7 ± 8.4 Ma) marks Cryogenian alkaline magmatism in the SGT and it is consistent with the evolution of the Madurai block. The Alkaline magmatism manifested in the Sholayar pluton is controlled by supra-subduction zone milieu that possibly involved slab-pull force during Mozambique ocean closure. Published: 2026-06-17T15:49:32+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.575 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Moumita Chowdhury; Jyotisankar Ray; Debaleena Sarkar; Suresh C. Patel; J. Amal Dev; Christian Koeberl; Anindita Dey; Desikan Ramakrishnan; Rohit Pandey; Pratik Mukherjee

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108667

Relevance: 0.575 (consider)

The Sholayar syenite pluton (10°17′59.9”N-10°18′39.8”N: 076°47′26.3″E-076°43′51.6″E) is located in the Madurai block of the Southern Granulite Terrane (SGT) within the Indian shield, and it is bounded by prominent shear zones in this region namely, Palghat Cauvery Shear Zone, Moyar Bhabani Shear Zone, and Achankovil Shear Zone. The Sholayar syenite stretches for about 8 km along the strike length in a WNW-ESE direction and consists of three main litho-members (namely leuco syenite, melanofelsic syenite and mela syenite) based on their petrographic characters. Geochemically, the syenite pluton exhibits a wide compositional spectrum ranging from alkalic, alkali-calcic, calcic-alkali and alkalic. Geochemical variation diagrams clearly indicate the role of differentiation from a parent magma that suffered fractional crystallization, being controlled by the constituent minerals (alkali feldspar, plagioclase, amphibole, biotite and clinopyroxene). The SrNd isotopic ratio shows a clear separation of the isotopic data sets between the mela syenite and leuco syenite + melanofelsic syenite groups. The mela syenites show higher values of εNd and lower initial Strontium-ratios, suggesting a cumulate nature which is also supported by the petrographic evidence. SrNd isotopic ratios suggest that the silica-oversaturated type was formed during crustal contamination accompanied by fractional crystallization from a parent silica- undersaturated (basanitic) stratified magma chamber, while the complementary silica- undersaturated syenite type is typically uncontaminated. Slab melting played an important role for generation of the parent magma from garnet/spinel lherzolite or Neoarchean/Paleoproterozoic protolith. The melting process was controlled by a convergent tectonic setting (close to subduction) as evident from the geochemistry and tectonic discrimination diagrams. The deduced UPb zircon age (706.7 ± 8.4 Ma) marks Cryogenian alkaline magmatism in the SGT and it is consistent with the evolution of the Madurai block. The Alkaline magmatism manifested in the Sholayar pluton is controlled by supra-subduction zone milieu that possibly involved slab-pull force during Mozambique ocean closure.

item.title: Pressure-temperature-time evolution of the Lüliang Group, North China craton: Insights from phase equilibria modeling and geochronology
item.dc:creator: Jiahui Qian
Changqing Yin
Jian Zhang
Peng Gao
Chengsen Lu
Shangjing Wu
item.description: Unraveling the tectono-thermal history of medium- to low-grade belts would place important constraints on the regional tectonic evolution. There exists a Barrovian metamorphic belt in the Lüliang Group, Trans-North China orogen of the North China craton. Representative rock samples from chlorite zone, biotite zone, garnet zone, and staurolite-kyanite zone have been collected to delineate the pressure-temperature-time (P-T-t) evolution. A two-stage prograde P-T path characterized by heating first and then pressurizing is recovered from the garnet zone by phase equilibria modeling. The peak P-T conditions are constrained to be ∼7.0 kbar and 560 °C. Decompression-dominated P-T paths involving peak pressure (6.8−9.2 kbar, 515−565 °C) and peak temperature (4.6−6.5 kbar, 560−615 °C) stages are obtained from the staurolite-kyanite zone. Metamorphic zircon from the staurolite-kyanite zone and garnet from the garnet zone yield U-Pb ages of 1850 ± 31 Ma and 1882 ± 67 Ma, respectively. Biotite from the biotite zone gives an 40Ar/39Ar age of 1762 ± 3 Ma. The geochronological results indicate that metamorphism of the Lüliang Group is younger than the formation age of the Trans-North China orogen (ca. 1.95 Ga) but is coeval with the subduction-collision orogeny (1.90−1.82 Ga) along the northern margin of the North China craton. The distribution of the Barrovian metamorphic belt is also parallel to the latter orogen (E-W trending). Consequently, combining these results with field observations and regional geological evolution, it is inferred that the genesis of Barrovian metamorphism in the Lüliang Group may be related to the stress propagated from the 1.90−1.82 Ga orogeny. The heat sources and formation mechanisms of Barrovian metamorphism globally may vary case by case. Published: 2026-06-18T21:05:44+00:00 Venue: Geological Society of America Bulletin Score: 0.575 (consider)

item.title: Multi-stage magmatic evolution and genetic model of carbonatite and syenite in the Yangyugou Complex, Central China: Insights from U–Pb geochronology and Nd isotopes
item.dc:creator: Ahmed S. Moftah
Fangyue Wang
Chen Ni
Liuan Duan
Long Zhang
Taofa Zhou
Yu Fan
item.description: Carbonatites and syenites are commonly associated in geological complexes, but their genetic relationship has not been fully understood. This study, by integrating chronology and isotopic geochemical analyses, provides new constraints on the traditionally held “cogenetic and contemporaneous carbonatite–syenite” model, suggesting that the two units in the Yangyugou Complex did not derive from the same source at the same time, and further reveals the magmatic evolutionary processes controlling their temporal and spatial association. We studied the Yangyugou Complex, located within the Qinling Orogenic Belt, through monazite and rutile U–Pb geochronology in carbonatites, as well as in-situ analyses of trace elements and Nd isotopic compositions in apatite and monazite in syenites and carbonatites, respectively. U–Pb geochronology of rutile and monazite from carbonatites indicates consistent crystallization ages of ∼ 240 Ma, corresponding to Late Triassic magmatic activity. There are significant differences in Nd isotopic compositions between apatite from syenites and monazite from carbonatites in the Yangyugou complex: the εNd(t) isotopic signatures of apatite extend from − 16.1 to − 10.8, while those of monazite range from –20.3 to –18.6, indicating an essential difference in their melt sources. Compared with other carbonatites in the Qinling Orogenic Belt, the Yangyugou carbonatite exhibits more negative εNd(t) values, indicating stronger involvement of crustal materials and/or subducted sediment-derived components. In this study, a new model is proposed in which the Yangyugou Complex in the Qinling Orogenic Belt experienced two distinct magmatic events. The first, Silurian syenite (∼438.9 ± 3.3 Ma), generated by lower crust partial melting and metasomatized mantle source, while the second, Late Triassic carbonatite magmatism mixed with REE- and CO2-enriched slab fluids and melt of deep-sea sediments (∼240 Ma), intruded along structural pathways into the pre-existing syenite. Their contrasting ages and sources indicate that the two rock types are genetically independent, reflecting the complex multi-stage magmatic evolution of the belt. Published: 2026-06-16T15:45:59+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.574 (consider)

Authors: Ahmed S. Moftah; Fangyue Wang; Chen Ni; Liuan Duan; Long Zhang; Taofa Zhou; Yu Fan

Journal: Ore Geology Reviews

DOI: 10.1016/j.oregeorev.2026.107376

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

Carbonatites and syenites are commonly associated in geological complexes, but their genetic relationship has not been fully understood. This study, by integrating chronology and isotopic geochemical analyses, provides new constraints on the traditionally held “cogenetic and contemporaneous carbonatite–syenite” model, suggesting that the two units in the Yangyugou Complex did not derive from the same source at the same time, and further reveals the magmatic evolutionary processes controlling their temporal and spatial association. We studied the Yangyugou Complex, located within the Qinling Orogenic Belt, through monazite and rutile U–Pb geochronology in carbonatites, as well as in-situ analyses of trace elements and Nd isotopic compositions in apatite and monazite in syenites and carbonatites, respectively. U–Pb geochronology of rutile and monazite from carbonatites indicates consistent crystallization ages of ∼ 240 Ma, corresponding to Late Triassic magmatic activity. There are significant differences in Nd isotopic compositions between apatite from syenites and monazite from carbonatites in the Yangyugou complex: the εNd(t) isotopic signatures of apatite extend from − 16.1 to − 10.8, while those of monazite range from –20.3 to –18.6, indicating an essential difference in their melt sources. Compared with other carbonatites in the Qinling Orogenic Belt, the Yangyugou carbonatite exhibits more negative εNd(t) values, indicating stronger involvement of crustal materials and/or subducted sediment-derived components. In this study, a new model is proposed in which the Yangyugou Complex in the Qinling Orogenic Belt experienced two distinct magmatic events. The first, Silurian syenite (∼438.9 ± 3.3 Ma), generated by lower crust partial melting and metasomatized mantle source, while the second, Late Triassic carbonatite magmatism mixed with REE- and CO2-enriched slab fluids and melt of deep-sea sediments (∼240 Ma), intruded along structural pathways into the pre-existing syenite. Their contrasting ages and sources indicate that the two rock types are genetically independent, reflecting the complex multi-stage magmatic evolution of the belt.

item.title: Interplay between supercontinents and accretionary orogens recorded in 200 million years of metallogeny
item.dc:creator: Cheng, Yanbo
Gilmore, Phil
Doublier, Michael
Czarnota, Karol
Lewis, Chris
Taylor, Holly
Clark, Andrew
Pitt, Liam
Mole, David
Wang, Yue
Meffre, Sebastien
Brotodewo, Adrienne
Cayley, Ross
Cairns, Cameron
Burnham, Antony
Wise, Tom
item.description: The assembly of supercontinents and the evolution of accretionary orogens are one of the most fundamental drivers of Earth's lithospheric architecture and mineral endowment. Here we reconstruct the 200 million years metallogenic history of the mainland Australian part of the Delamerian convergent margin, a pivotal segment of the East Gondwana paleo-Pacific margin that archives the transition from Rodinian fragmentation to terminal Gondwana assembly. Our analysis reveals that the region's mineral potential is a non-random result of sequential tectonic switching and specific magmatic priming. Magmatic fertility was governed by a narrow window of high hydration, deep-seated fractionation, and elevated oxidation states driven by the recycling of continental and oceanic sediments during multiple tectonic switching events. Integrated geological, geochemical, geochronological, and sulphur isotope evidence identifies four temporally distinct mineralisation events – from Neoproterozoic orthomagmatic Ni-Cu to Siluro-Devonian intrusion-related Au formations. Unlike the specialised “copper factories” of the Andes or the reduced magmatic systems of Japan, the Delamerian convergent margin functions as a diverse “general store” of strategic resources and critical minerals preserved beneath widespread post-mineralisation sedimentary cover. This 200 million years record demonstrates how the interactions between supercontinent and accretionary orogens are translated into four distinctive metallogenic belts within one coherent mineral province. Published: 2026-06-21T08:48:00+00:00 Venue: EarthArXiv Score: 0.574 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Cheng, Yanbo; Gilmore, Phil; Doublier, Michael; Czarnota, Karol; Lewis, Chris; Taylor, Holly; Clark, Andrew; Pitt, Liam; Mole, David; Wang, Yue; Meffre, Sebastien; Brotodewo, Adrienne; Cayley, Ross; Cairns, Cameron; Burnham, Antony; Wise, Tom

Journal: EarthArXiv

DOI: 10.31223/X5819N

Relevance: 0.574 (consider)

The assembly of supercontinents and the evolution of accretionary orogens are one of the most fundamental drivers of Earth's lithospheric architecture and mineral endowment. Here we reconstruct the 200 million years metallogenic history of the mainland Australian part of the Delamerian convergent margin, a pivotal segment of the East Gondwana paleo-Pacific margin that archives the transition from Rodinian fragmentation to terminal Gondwana assembly. Our analysis reveals that the region's mineral potential is a non-random result of sequential tectonic switching and specific magmatic priming. Magmatic fertility was governed by a narrow window of high hydration, deep-seated fractionation, and elevated oxidation states driven by the recycling of continental and oceanic sediments during multiple tectonic switching events. Integrated geological, geochemical, geochronological, and sulphur isotope evidence identifies four temporally distinct mineralisation events – from Neoproterozoic orthomagmatic Ni-Cu to Siluro-Devonian intrusion-related Au formations. Unlike the specialised “copper factories” of the Andes or the reduced magmatic systems of Japan, the Delamerian convergent margin functions as a diverse “general store” of strategic resources and critical minerals preserved beneath widespread post-mineralisation sedimentary cover. This 200 million years record demonstrates how the interactions between supercontinent and accretionary orogens are translated into four distinctive metallogenic belts within one coherent mineral province.

item.title: Dehydration of metasedimentary rocks triggers early water-flux melting of metagranitoids and influences strain localization
item.dc:creator: Jonas Vanardois
Pierre Lanari
item.description: Abstract Large melt volumes in metagranitoid lithologies are described in numerous orogens and often play a major role in the strain distribution within the orogenic crust. In a continental crust devoid of free water, melt fractions above 10 vol% in metagranitoid rocks can only be generated by the influx of external water via a fluid phase. One possible origin of these aqueous fluids is the subsolidus dehydration of the surrounding metasedimentary rocks. To test this hypothesis, a path-dependent multi-system phase equilibrium model was developed. This model simulates the amount of aqueous fluid produced by a metasedimentary system in the temperature interval between the solidus of the orthogneiss and that of the metasediment. This amount of aqueous fluid is transferred to the orthogneiss system, and the new melt fraction is calculated. This simulation was carried out along eight prograde Pressure–Temperature (P–T) paths using metapelite/orthogneiss and metagraywacke/orthogneiss associations. The results show that metasedimentary rocks produce between 0.01–0.2 wt.% of aqueous fluid due to the breakdown of staurolite and paragonite, and the minor consumption of muscovite and biotite. Nevertheless, the small amount of aqueous fluid increases the melt fractions of the orthogneiss system by several percent, making orthogneiss the most fertile lithology along most prograde paths at temperatures below 750°C and pressures from 0.5 to 1.5 GPa. If less than half of the crust is composed of orthogneiss, the volume of melt generated in the orthogneiss is sufficient to significantly weaken it, allowing deformation to localize within the orthogneiss bodies. We propose that this strain localization situation creates preferential pathways for additional fluid influxes, which enhances partial melting in metagranitoid lithologies and creates a positive feedback loop. Our results suggest that this process may occur in most prograde P–T paths in orogenic crusts composed of metasedimentary rocks and orthogneisses. Published: 2026-06-16T12:38:21+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.573 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Jonas Vanardois; Pierre Lanari

Journal: Journal of Petrology

DOI: 10.1093/petrology/egag052

Relevance: 0.573 (consider)

Abstract Large melt volumes in metagranitoid lithologies are described in numerous orogens and often play a major role in the strain distribution within the orogenic crust. In a continental crust devoid of free water, melt fractions above 10 vol% in metagranitoid rocks can only be generated by the influx of external water via a fluid phase. One possible origin of these aqueous fluids is the subsolidus dehydration of the surrounding metasedimentary rocks. To test this hypothesis, a path-dependent multi-system phase equilibrium model was developed. This model simulates the amount of aqueous fluid produced by a metasedimentary system in the temperature interval between the solidus of the orthogneiss and that of the metasediment. This amount of aqueous fluid is transferred to the orthogneiss system, and the new melt fraction is calculated. This simulation was carried out along eight prograde Pressure–Temperature (P–T) paths using metapelite/orthogneiss and metagraywacke/orthogneiss associations. The results show that metasedimentary rocks produce between 0.01–0.2 wt.% of aqueous fluid due to the breakdown of staurolite and paragonite, and the minor consumption of muscovite and biotite. Nevertheless, the small amount of aqueous fluid increases the melt fractions of the orthogneiss system by several percent, making orthogneiss the most fertile lithology along most prograde paths at temperatures below 750°C and pressures from 0.5 to 1.5 GPa. If less than half of the crust is composed of orthogneiss, the volume of melt generated in the orthogneiss is sufficient to significantly weaken it, allowing deformation to localize within the orthogneiss bodies. We propose that this strain localization situation creates preferential pathways for additional fluid influxes, which enhances partial melting in metagranitoid lithologies and creates a positive feedback loop. Our results suggest that this process may occur in most prograde P–T paths in orogenic crusts composed of metasedimentary rocks and orthogneisses.

item.title: The Lar alkaline igneous complex: Age, mineralogy, chemistry, and isotopes of a potassic post-collisional event in the Eastern Iran Magmatic Belt (Sistan Suture Zone, East Iran)
item.dc:creator: M. Ghiotto
C. Natali
A. Rabiee
A. Bragagni
E. Braschi
R. Avanzinelli
M. Casalini
S. Ghafaribijar
M. Arvin
F. Rossetti
S. Conticelli
item.description: This study investigates the Cenozoic magma genesis in the East Iran Magmatic Belt through a detailed petrographic, geochemical, and radiometric analysis of igneous rocks from the Lar alkaline complex in the Sistan Suture Zone (East Iran). The Lar complex consists of intrusive and hypabyssal rocks showing shoshonitic to ultrapotassic affinities with variable degrees of silica saturation. The most alkaline silica-undersaturated lithotypes include lamprophyres, nepheline-syenites, and felsic dykes of phonolitic composition, whereas silica-saturated and over-saturated rocks are represented by syenites, trachytic dykes and monzonites. U-Pb and K-Ar geochronological constraints indicate that intrusive and hypabyssal igneous rocks of the Lar complex were generated in a time span of ca. 3 Ma in the Early Oligocene (from 32.5 ± 0.4 to 29.3 ± 0.6 Ma). Lamprophyres and nepheline-syenites show pseudoleucite textures due to subsolidus unmixing forming nepheline and K-feldspar intergrowths. Incompatible trace element patterns show geochemical features compatible with a subduction-related setting (i.e., large ion lithophile elements -LILE- enrichments and high field strength elements -HFSE- depletions), with the alkaline potassic and silica-undersaturated rocks characterized by higher light/heavy rare earth elements (LREE/HREE) fractionation (LaN/YbN from 12 to 22) with respect to the silica-saturated and -oversaturated rocks (LaN/YbN from 10 to 12). The initial Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic composition of the Lar igneous rocks is rather homogeneous (87Sr/86Sri = 0.704295–0.706222, 143Nd/144Ndi = 0.512627–0.512748, 206Pb/204Pbi = 18.82–18.90, 207Pb/204 Pbi = 15.62–15.65, 208Pb/204Pbi = 39.00–39.16) suggesting a common mantle source and limited crustal contamination/assimilation, which only affects the most differentiated products. The geochemical and isotopic features of the Lar magmas suggest that mantle metasomatism occurred as partial melts derived mainly from carbonate-rich sediments. The formation of the Lar igneous complex is attributed to post-collisional magmatism. This magmatism was triggered by asthenospheric upwelling that caused melting within the overlying mantle. Crucially, the mantle composition had been modified (metasomatized) by materials derived from the subducted Sistan Neo-Tethyan oceanic slab. Published: 2026-06-21T14:34:00+00:00 Venue: Lithos Score: 0.571 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: M. Ghiotto; C. Natali; A. Rabiee; A. Bragagni; E. Braschi; R. Avanzinelli; M. Casalini; S. Ghafaribijar; M. Arvin; F. Rossetti; S. Conticelli

Journal: Lithos

DOI: 10.1016/j.lithos.2026.108681

Relevance: 0.571 (consider)

This study investigates the Cenozoic magma genesis in the East Iran Magmatic Belt through a detailed petrographic, geochemical, and radiometric analysis of igneous rocks from the Lar alkaline complex in the Sistan Suture Zone (East Iran). The Lar complex consists of intrusive and hypabyssal rocks showing shoshonitic to ultrapotassic affinities with variable degrees of silica saturation. The most alkaline silica-undersaturated lithotypes include lamprophyres, nepheline-syenites, and felsic dykes of phonolitic composition, whereas silica-saturated and over-saturated rocks are represented by syenites, trachytic dykes and monzonites. U-Pb and K-Ar geochronological constraints indicate that intrusive and hypabyssal igneous rocks of the Lar complex were generated in a time span of ca. 3 Ma in the Early Oligocene (from 32.5 ± 0.4 to 29.3 ± 0.6 Ma). Lamprophyres and nepheline-syenites show pseudoleucite textures due to subsolidus unmixing forming nepheline and K-feldspar intergrowths. Incompatible trace element patterns show geochemical features compatible with a subduction-related setting (i.e., large ion lithophile elements -LILE- enrichments and high field strength elements -HFSE- depletions), with the alkaline potassic and silica-undersaturated rocks characterized by higher light/heavy rare earth elements (LREE/HREE) fractionation (LaN/YbN from 12 to 22) with respect to the silica-saturated and -oversaturated rocks (LaN/YbN from 10 to 12). The initial Sr-Nd-Pb isotopic composition of the Lar igneous rocks is rather homogeneous (87Sr/86Sri = 0.704295–0.706222, 143Nd/144Ndi = 0.512627–0.512748, 206Pb/204Pbi = 18.82–18.90, 207Pb/204 Pbi = 15.62–15.65, 208Pb/204Pbi = 39.00–39.16) suggesting a common mantle source and limited crustal contamination/assimilation, which only affects the most differentiated products. The geochemical and isotopic features of the Lar magmas suggest that mantle metasomatism occurred as partial melts derived mainly from carbonate-rich sediments. The formation of the Lar igneous complex is attributed to post-collisional magmatism. This magmatism was triggered by asthenospheric upwelling that caused melting within the overlying mantle. Crucially, the mantle composition had been modified (metasomatized) by materials derived from the subducted Sistan Neo-Tethyan oceanic slab.

item.title: Punctuated energetics of dehydration contributes to slow earthquake phenomena
item.dc:creator: Timothy Chapman
Sandra Piazolo
Geoffrey Clarke
Lars Hansen
Manon Carpenter
item.description: The prograde breakdown of hydrous minerals like lawsonite is energy-intensive and releases fluids at depths coincident with extensive slow earthquake phenomena for many subduction geotherms. However, there remains inconsistency between the spatial occurrence and inferred temporal rates of these dehydration reactions relative to the episodic character of slow earthquakes. The example of lawsonite breakdown shows that dehydration reactions will dictate heat consumption, thus buffering the rate of temperature increase for a rock undergoing intervals of metamorphism (<30 °C) and delaying each small increment (∼1 °C) of fluid release. A nonlinear behavior is predicted involving periods of fast fluid release paused by periods of slower heat absorption. Such stepped reaction progress is consistent with natural rock textures of lawsonite breakdown indicating intermittent fluid-present crack propagation and associated step changes in mineral growth. Pulsed fluid flow into rocks of heterogeneous permeability may result in stress build-up enabling crack propagation and slip at different scales. Energetics of punctuated dehydration and resultant fluid flow match temporal and spatial characteristics of slow slip behavior. Consequently, episodic dehydration is suggested as a viable trigger for slow slip. Published: 2026-06-18T18:52:10+00:00 Venue: Geology Score: 0.571 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Timothy Chapman; Sandra Piazolo; Geoffrey Clarke; Lars Hansen; Manon Carpenter

Journal: Geology

DOI: 10.1130/g54149.1

Relevance: 0.571 (consider)

The prograde breakdown of hydrous minerals like lawsonite is energy-intensive and releases fluids at depths coincident with extensive slow earthquake phenomena for many subduction geotherms. However, there remains inconsistency between the spatial occurrence and inferred temporal rates of these dehydration reactions relative to the episodic character of slow earthquakes. The example of lawsonite breakdown shows that dehydration reactions will dictate heat consumption, thus buffering the rate of temperature increase for a rock undergoing intervals of metamorphism (<30 °C) and delaying each small increment (∼1 °C) of fluid release. A nonlinear behavior is predicted involving periods of fast fluid release paused by periods of slower heat absorption. Such stepped reaction progress is consistent with natural rock textures of lawsonite breakdown indicating intermittent fluid-present crack propagation and associated step changes in mineral growth. Pulsed fluid flow into rocks of heterogeneous permeability may result in stress build-up enabling crack propagation and slip at different scales. Energetics of punctuated dehydration and resultant fluid flow match temporal and spatial characteristics of slow slip behavior. Consequently, episodic dehydration is suggested as a viable trigger for slow slip.

item.title: Fluid sources and evolution of early Oligocene Be-W deposits in the Mahuaping ore district, Western Yunnan
item.dc:creator: Sheng Li
Wenchang Li
Fucheng Yang
Qinggao Yan
Huawen Cao
Shenjin Guan
Jiachun Wen
Guiyin Zhang
Guizhong Yang
item.description: The Cenozoic Sanjiang Tethyan metallogenic belt is renowned for large-scale porphyry-skarn Cu-Mo-Au mineralization systems. Recent studies have shown that this belt also host coeval Be and W mineralization, however, the genesis and evolution mechanisms of such coeval critical metal (e.g., W, Be) mineralization remain poorly constrained. This study focuses on the Yinchangping quartz-vein type Be-W deposit, which occurs within ophiolitic mélange in the Mahuaping ore district, western Yunnan. Through in situ muscovite Rb-Sr dating and scheelite geochemical analysis, the ore-forming age is precisely constrained at 29.5 ± 1.3 Ma (Early Oligocene). Scheelite display relatively uniform 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.7056 – 0.7085), significant Y/Ho fractionation (19.1 – 59.8), and non-charge-and-radius-controlled (non-CHARAC) behavior, suggesting that the ubiquitous positive Eu anomalies and absence of Ce anomalies in scheelite collectively record a persistently reducing hydrothermal environment. The decoupling of in-situ Sr isotopes and Sr contents, accompanied by high-frequency ’sawtooth’ fluctuations and core-to-rim isotopic profile characteristics, robustly demonstrates that driven by a ’fault-valve’ mechanism, pristine magmatic fluids underwent multi-component mixing with highly radiogenic fluids that had intensively interacted with the basement, mid-ocean ridge basalt (MORB) components, or carbonate wall rocks. This intense water–rock interaction thoroughly disrupted the fluid chemical equilibrium, thereby triggering the large-scale co-precipitation of scheelite and beryl. More importantly, this study reveals that the Yinchangping and adjacent Mahuaping deposits are not isolated occurrences. Instead, they likely belong to the same magmatic‑hydrothermal metallogenic system. As a proximal end-member, the Yinchangping deposit directly records the early stage process of deep magmatic fluid ascent to shallow levels, undergoing water–rock interaction and initial mixing with metamorphic fluids. With the continuous upward evolution of fluids, the distal Mahuaping deposit experienced more intensive metamorphism, leading to attenuation of magmatic signals and completion of a geochemical transition to metamorphic fluid dominance. These two deposits constitute a temporally and spatially continuous Early Oligocene “magmatic-metamorphic transitional” Be-W metallogenic system within the Mahuaping ore district. Published: 2026-06-18T15:54:32+00:00 Venue: Ore Geology Reviews Score: 0.570 (consider)

Relevance: 0.570 (consider)

The Cenozoic Sanjiang Tethyan metallogenic belt is renowned for large-scale porphyry-skarn Cu-Mo-Au mineralization systems. Recent studies have shown that this belt also host coeval Be and W mineralization, however, the genesis and evolution mechanisms of such coeval critical metal (e.g., W, Be) mineralization remain poorly constrained. This study focuses on the Yinchangping quartz-vein type Be-W deposit, which occurs within ophiolitic mélange in the Mahuaping ore district, western Yunnan. Through in situ muscovite Rb-Sr dating and scheelite geochemical analysis, the ore-forming age is precisely constrained at 29.5 ± 1.3 Ma (Early Oligocene). Scheelite display relatively uniform 87Sr/86Sr ratios (0.7056 – 0.7085), significant Y/Ho fractionation (19.1 – 59.8), and non-charge-and-radius-controlled (non-CHARAC) behavior, suggesting that the ubiquitous positive Eu anomalies and absence of Ce anomalies in scheelite collectively record a persistently reducing hydrothermal environment. The decoupling of in-situ Sr isotopes and Sr contents, accompanied by high-frequency ’sawtooth’ fluctuations and core-to-rim isotopic profile characteristics, robustly demonstrates that driven by a ’fault-valve’ mechanism, pristine magmatic fluids underwent multi-component mixing with highly radiogenic fluids that had intensively interacted with the basement, mid-ocean ridge basalt (MORB) components, or carbonate wall rocks. This intense water–rock interaction thoroughly disrupted the fluid chemical equilibrium, thereby triggering the large-scale co-precipitation of scheelite and beryl. More importantly, this study reveals that the Yinchangping and adjacent Mahuaping deposits are not isolated occurrences. Instead, they likely belong to the same magmatic‑hydrothermal metallogenic system. As a proximal end-member, the Yinchangping deposit directly records the early stage process of deep magmatic fluid ascent to shallow levels, undergoing water–rock interaction and initial mixing with metamorphic fluids. With the continuous upward evolution of fluids, the distal Mahuaping deposit experienced more intensive metamorphism, leading to attenuation of magmatic signals and completion of a geochemical transition to metamorphic fluid dominance. These two deposits constitute a temporally and spatially continuous Early Oligocene “magmatic-metamorphic transitional” Be-W metallogenic system within the Mahuaping ore district.

item.title: CO2-rich intraplate alkaline magmatism in the Levant: melt inclusion insights into source-to-surface evolution of the Ramon volcanics, Israel
item.dc:creator: Haran Hennig
Yevgeny Vapnik
Zinobi Yudalevich
Sharon Hazan
Bar Elisha
Noriko T Kita
Yaron Katzir
item.description: Abstract Continental alkaline magmatic provinces are notable for their exceptional compositional diversity, making them valuable recorders of mantle source heterogeneity, magma differentiation processes, and volatile behaviour. Some of these provinces occur in intraplate settings where evidence for rifting or plume activity is absent, leaving the cause of mantle melting and magma ascent enigmatic. Here, olivine-hosted melt inclusions provide insight into the source-to-surface evolution of the Ramon volcanics, the most diverse series of the Early Cretaceous intraplate Levant magmatic province, whose origin is enigmatic. Most data are derived from the well-preserved nephelinite-basanite-alkaline basalt series of the Ga’ash Hill edifice, complemented by additional exposures in Makhtesh Ramon, Negev Desert, Israel. Trace-element systematics indicate that compositional diversity is mostly dominated by low-degree melting of a garnet-bearing mantle source near the garnet-spinel transition, with contributions from peridotite and pyroxenite lithologies. Melt inclusions are CO2-rich (≤ 3.19 wt.%), while H2O contents are moderate with reconstructed values of up to 0.95 wt.%. Volatile saturation pressures recorded in melt inclusions span a wide range (1300–230 MPa), further constrained by clinopyroxene geobarometry to indicate magma storage at lower-crustal levels (~25–35 km), near the reconstructed Moho. Fluid-behaviour modelling suggests a two-stage volatile control on melt mobilization: CO2 dominating deep transport and H2O becoming increasingly important at shallow levels below ~ 250 MPa, where eruptibility increases. Minimal interaction between ascending melts and crustal rocks is indicated by oxygen isotope ratios of olivine phenocrysts. Compositional diversity stems from the melting of a fertile lithospheric mantle source previously affected by carbonate-rich metasomatism, deep storage, and rapid ascent. Small perturbations in tectonic or thermal conditions may be sufficient to generate alkaline mafic melts from readily fusible carbonate- and pyroxenite-bearing lithospheric mantle, thus providing a possible explanation for magmatism in stable continental interiors that lack structural evidence for extension. Published: 2026-06-16T12:38:19+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.569 (consider)

Abstract Continental alkaline magmatic provinces are notable for their exceptional compositional diversity, making them valuable recorders of mantle source heterogeneity, magma differentiation processes, and volatile behaviour. Some of these provinces occur in intraplate settings where evidence for rifting or plume activity is absent, leaving the cause of mantle melting and magma ascent enigmatic. Here, olivine-hosted melt inclusions provide insight into the source-to-surface evolution of the Ramon volcanics, the most diverse series of the Early Cretaceous intraplate Levant magmatic province, whose origin is enigmatic. Most data are derived from the well-preserved nephelinite-basanite-alkaline basalt series of the Ga’ash Hill edifice, complemented by additional exposures in Makhtesh Ramon, Negev Desert, Israel. Small perturbations in tectonic or thermal conditions may be sufficient to generate alkaline mafic melts from readily fusible carbonate- and pyroxenite-bearing lithospheric mantle, thus providing a possible explanation for magmatism in stable continental interiors that lack structural evidence for extension.
